title: Designing a Garden
rationale: The Agenda consists of reading, garden design, drawing, and sharing. The closing tasting is only sampling, with no hands-on cooking or structured tasting practice.

title: Tastes Around the World
rationale: Students taste and smell foods at six stations, record sensory observations, and formally compare foods using taste, smell, appearance, sound, and texture. The food is prepared beforehand, so no preparation techniques are tagged.

title: Ancient China Bingo
rationale: Students read, examine a map, and play bingo. The closing tasting is only a sample and does not teach a cooking or structured tasting skill.

title: Food from the Eastern Hemisphere
rationale: The Agenda explicitly has students prepare fried rice, which maps to sautéing and stir-frying. Although oven, knives, graters, and measuring tools are listed, the Agenda does not establish the corresponding student techniques.

title: Fruit Salad Challenge
rationale: Teams compose fruit salads, with the lesson guidance directing them to cut fruit into bite-sized pieces. Students then compare every salad using tasting words and may conduct a blind taste test; dressing is only an optional suggestion.

title: Go, Grow, Glow
rationale: Students prepare the rice, beans, and salsa components of a burrito bowl, making dish assembly the clearly supported skill. The Agenda does not specify the cutting or stovetop techniques used for those components.

title: 5th Grade Food Cultures Unit Overview
rationale: The overview says students prepare a changing recipe in each lesson but does not identify any technique consistently practiced by students. Handwashing and sanitizer are routine transitions rather than explicit safety instruction.

title: Chickpea Salad
rationale: Students chop celery and peppers, make dressing by shaking tahini with the other dressing ingredients, and mix the dressed salad. The ingredients are pre-measured, so Measuring is not tagged.

title: Pico de Gallo (Knife Skills)
rationale: The Agenda explicitly teaches knife cuts and knife-safety practices before students cut vegetables. Students use those techniques to make pico de gallo, which the lesson identifies as a salsa or sauce.

title: African American Foodways: Hoppin' John
rationale: Students explicitly dice peppers, celery, and onions, while third graders make cornbread batter. The oven and stovetop are listed, but the Agenda does not show students themselves practicing a specific baking, sautéing, or simmering technique.

title: Vegetable Ramen
rationale: The cooking block directs students to boil noodles, mince and chop vegetables, grate ginger/carrots/cabbage, measure corn, and make furikake seasoning. Setting up a toppings bar does not clearly show students assembling individual dishes.

title: Healthy Snacks (Energy Bites, Popcorn, Cracker Stack)
rationale: Students roll energy bites, season and shake popcorn to mix it, and assemble cracker stacks. They use tasting vocabulary to describe and compare three snacks and vote for a favorite; the tablespoon is used for portioning rather than clearly taught measurement.

title: 3rd Grade Chinese-Style Dumplings
rationale: Students wash hands and review the Kitchen Pledge, then cut scallions, mash soybeans, measure seasonings, grate several ingredients, mix the filling, and fill/fold/seal wrappers. Steaming is excluded because the Agenda assigns that operation to the teacher.

title: A Visit to Mashama Bailey's Restaurant
rationale: One group seasons and stirs the precooked grits, while another prepares stewed collard greens using a pot and induction burner. Onion dicing is completed during prep, and the Agenda does not explicitly assign measuring or grating to students.

title: Bees
rationale: Students make puppets, role-play pollination, and plant flower seeds. The honey tasting is only sampling and does not teach a cooking or structured tasting skill.

title: Recycle Gardening
rationale: The lesson is entirely about planting seeds in recycled containers and completing garden jobs. No hands-on food preparation occurs.

title: Seeds
rationale: Students perform a seed dance and remove seeds from pods for saving. The closing seed tasting is unstructured sampling rather than tasting instruction.

title: edibleschoolyards_planting
rationale: Students observe, measure, plant, and water garden plants. There is no cooking block or food preparation.

title: 2nd Grade Decomposition Experiment Part 1
rationale: Students set up decomposition experiments using soil, compost, and test items. The closing tasting is incidental and includes no cooking or sensory instruction.

title: Gardening & The Environment
rationale: Students rotate through planting, weeding, harvesting, composting, and watering stations. Eating an in-season fruit tasting at the end does not constitute cooking or structured tasting practice.

title: The Garden in the Spring
rationale: Students search the garden for signs of spring and discuss their observations. No food preparation or tasting activity appears in the Agenda.

title: September Sixth Grade Summer Plant Hunt
rationale: The Agenda explicitly teaches a sensory tasting procedure, asks students to observe with smell, touch, and sight, and then assess whether the sample is sweet or sour. The remainder of the lesson is garden exploration and watering.

title: Food of Peru
rationale: Students prepare arroz chaufa, identified in the Agenda as fried rice, which maps directly to sautéing and stir-frying. Chicha is boiled before class, while the other listed tools do not establish additional student techniques.

title: Foods From Around the World: China
rationale: Each group explicitly follows the same smashed-cucumber recipe, cutting cucumbers, grating or pressing garlic, and mixing a dressing before adding it to the drained cucumbers. Students finish by using tasting words; smashing is not tagged as Mashing because the lesson specifically avoids reducing the cucumbers to a mash.

title: Expert's Guide to Gardening: Compost
rationale: Students observe worms and compost and complete a gardening workbook page. The seasonal tasting is merely a closing sample, with no hands-on cooking or structured sensory exercise.
